pool.q: Silicon is best categorized as a:
pool.desc: Silicon has intermediate properties, making it a metalloid.

pool.q: What is the correct chemical symbol for Potassium?
pool.desc: Potassium takes its symbol K from its Latin name Kalium.

pool.q: Which of the following explains Argon's lack of chemical reactivity?
opts: It has equal numbers of protons and neutrons
It has a low boiling point
pool.desc: Argon has 8 valence electrons (a full octet), making it a stable, unreactive noble gas.

pool.q: What charge does an Oxygen (O) ion typically form?
pool.desc: Oxygen is in Group 6. It gains 2 electrons to complete its octet, forming an O2- ion.

pool.q: Which of these elements belongs to Group 7 (The Halogens)?
pool.desc: Chlorine is a halogen in Group 7.

pool.q: Copper is classified as which type of element?
pool.desc: Copper sits in the central d-block, making it a transition metal.

pool.q: Which of the following is a noble gas found in Period 1?
pool.desc: Helium is the only noble gas located in Period 1.

pool.q: What charge does a Sodium (Na) ion typically form?
pool.desc: Sodium is in Group 1, so it loses 1 valence electron to achieve a full outer shell, forming a Na+ ion.

pool.q: Which property is highly typical of transition metals like Iron?
pool.desc: Transition metals typically exhibit variable oxidation states.

pool.q: When Magnesium (Mg) reacts with Oxygen, what type of bond is formed?
pool.desc: Magnesium (metal) transfers electrons to Oxygen (non-metal), forming an ionic bond.

pool.q: What charge does a Chlorine (Cl) ion typically form?
pool.desc: Chlorine is in Group 7 (Halogens) and gains 1 electron to form a Cl- ion.

pool.q: Which group does Phosphorus belong to?
pool.desc: Phosphorus shares Group 5 with Nitrogen.

pool.q: What is the charge of an Aluminum (Al) ion?
pool.desc: Aluminum is in Group 3, so it loses 3 electrons to achieve stability, forming Al3+.

pool.q: Why is Neon (Ne) completely unreactive?
opts: It has a full outer electron shell
It is a gas at room temperature
pool.a: It has a full outer electron shell
pool.desc: Noble gases like Neon have a completely full valence shell (stable octet), so they do not need to lose, gain, or share electrons.

pool.q: Which element has the atomic number 8?
pool.desc: Oxygen has 8 protons, making its atomic number 8.

pool.q: Which metal is uniquely liquid at room temperature?
pool.desc: Mercury (Hg) is the only metal that remains liquid at standard room temperature.

pool.q: What is the physical state and appearance of Bromine at room temperature?
pool.desc: Bromine is a volatile red-brown liquid at room temperature.

pool.q: What type of bonding does Carbon (C) typically form in compounds like CO2?
pool.desc: Carbon is a non-metal and typically shares electrons to form covalent bonds.

pool.q: What is the atomic number of Chlorine?
pool.desc: Chlorine has exactly 17 protons.

pool.q: Which element is represented by the symbol Al?
pool.desc: The symbol Al stands for Aluminium.

pool.q: What is the atomic number of Beryllium?
pool.desc: Beryllium has exactly 4 protons.

pool.q: What is the atomic number of Sodium?
pool.desc: Sodium has exactly 11 protons.

pool.desc: Fluorine is at the top of Group 7.

pool.q: What is the atomic number of Neon?
pool.desc: Neon has exactly 10 protons.

pool.desc: Magnesium is represented by the symbol Mg.

pool.desc: Neon is represented as Ne.

pool.q: What is the atomic number of Fluorine?
pool.desc: Fluorine has exactly 9 protons.

pool.desc: Sulfur is located in Group 6, Period 3.

pool.desc: Nitrogen follows Carbon with an atomic number of 7.

pool.desc: Helium has exactly 2 protons.

pool.desc: The symbol Ne stands for Neon.

pool.desc: Argon is represented by the symbol Ar.

pool.desc: The symbol H stands for Hydrogen.

pool.desc: Nitrogen is represented by the symbol N.

pool.desc: Magnesium has exactly 12 protons.

pool.desc: The symbol Ca stands for Calcium.

pool.desc: Hydrogen has exactly 1 protons.

pool.desc: Lithium is represented by the symbol Li.

pool.desc: Magnesium is represented by Mg.

pool.desc: Potassium is represented by the symbol K.

pool.q: What is the chemical symbol for Hydrogen?
pool.desc: Hydrogen is represented by the symbol H.

pool.q: Write the chemical symbol for Calcium.
pool.desc: Calcium's symbol is Ca.

pool.desc: Lithium has an atomic number of 3.

pool.desc: The symbol B stands for Boron.

pool.desc: The symbol Cl stands for Chlorine.

pool.desc: Phosphorus is represented by the symbol P.

pool.desc: Carbon has exactly 6 protons in its nucleus.

pool.desc: Beryllium is represented by the symbol Be.

pool.desc: Aluminium has 13 protons.

pool.desc: Boron is represented by the symbol B.

pool.desc: Sulfur has exactly 16 protons.

pool.desc: Lithium has exactly 3 protons.

pool.desc: Oxygen has exactly 8 protons.

pool.q: What is the atomic number of Calcium?
pool.desc: Calcium has exactly 20 protons.

pool.q: Which element is represented by the symbol Mg?
pool.desc: The symbol Mg stands for Magnesium.

pool.q: Which element is represented by the symbol Be?
pool.desc: The symbol Be stands for Beryllium.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,8,6?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,8,6 sums to 16 electrons, which is Sulfur.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,8,8?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,8,8 sums to 18 electrons, which is Argon.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of an Aluminium atom?
pool.desc: Aluminium has 13 electrons arranged as 2,8,3.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,8,5?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,8,5 sums to 15 electrons, which is Phosphorus.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,5?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,5 sums to 7 electrons, which is Nitrogen.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Lithium atom?
pool.desc: Lithium has 3 electrons arranged as 2,1.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Sulfur atom?
pool.desc: Sulfur has 16 electrons arranged as 2,8,6.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Silicon atom?
pool.desc: Silicon has 14 electrons arranged as 2,8,4.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Fluorine atom?
pool.desc: Fluorine has 9 electrons arranged as 2,7.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 1?
pool.desc: The configuration 1 sums to 1 electrons, which is Hydrogen.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Potassium atom?
pool.desc: Potassium has 19 electrons arranged as 2,8,8,1.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Chlorine atom?
pool.desc: Chlorine has 17 electrons arranged as 2,8,7.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,8,1?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,8,1 sums to 11 electrons, which is Sodium.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Oxygen atom?
pool.desc: Oxygen has 8 total electrons distributed as 2,6.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Argon atom?
pool.desc: Argon has 18 electrons arranged as 2,8,8.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Carbon atom?
pool.desc: Carbon has 6 electrons arranged as 2,4.

pool.q: Which neutral element corresponds to the electron configuration 2,8,7?
pool.desc: 2+8+7 = 17 electrons, which is Chlorine.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a Fluorine atom?
pool.desc: Fluorine has 9 total electrons arranged as 2,7.

pool.q: How many valence (outermost) electrons does a Nitrogen atom possess?
pool.desc: Nitrogen (2,5) has 5 outer valence electrons.

pool.q: How many fully occupied electron shells does a neutral Magnesium atom (2,8,2) have?
pool.desc: The first two shells are completely full (2 and 8).

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Magnesium atom?
pool.desc: Magnesium has 12 electrons arranged as 2,8,2.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a Calcium atom (Atomic Number = 20)?
pool.desc: Calcium's 20 electrons fill the shells up to 2,8,8,2.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,8,7?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,8,7 sums to 17 electrons, which is Chlorine.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,2?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,2 sums to 4 electrons, which is Beryllium.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Sodium atom?
pool.desc: Sodium has 11 electrons arranged as 2,8,1.

pool.q: What is the standard electron configuration of a neutral Sodium atom?
pool.desc: Sodium has 11 electrons: 2, 8, and 1 valence electron.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,1?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,1 sums to 3 electrons, which is Lithium.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,8,2?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,8,2 sums to 12 electrons, which is Magnesium.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Aluminium atom?
pool.desc: Aluminium has 13 electrons arranged as 2,8,3.

pool.q: Which element has the electron configuration 2,7?
pool.desc: The configuration 2,7 sums to 9 electrons, which is Fluorine.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Helium atom?
pool.desc: Helium has 2 electrons arranged as 2.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Calcium atom?
pool.desc: Calcium has 20 electrons arranged as 2,8,8,2.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of Argon (Atomic Number = 18)?
pool.desc: Argon has a stable full outer shell configuration of 2,8,8.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a Lithium atom?
pool.desc: Lithium has 3 total electrons arranged as 2,1.

pool.q: What is the electron configuration of a neutral Boron atom?
pool.desc: Boron has 5 electrons arranged as 2,3.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 1, Group 1?
pool.desc: Hydrogen has 1 shells and 1 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 2?
pool.desc: Beryllium has 2 shells and 2 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 2?
pool.desc: Magnesium has 3 shells and 2 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 4?
pool.desc: Silicon has 3 shells and 4 valence electrons.

pool.q: In which Group number is Sodium found?
pool.desc: Sodium is an alkali metal in Group 1.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 1?
pool.desc: Sodium has 3 shells and 1 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 8?
pool.desc: Neon has 2 shells and 8 valence electrons.

pool.q: In which Group number is Chlorine found?
pool.desc: Chlorine is located in Group 7 (Halogens).

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 7?
pool.desc: Chlorine has 3 shells and 7 valence electrons.

pool.q: In which Group number is Carbon located?
pool.desc: Carbon sits at the head of Group 4.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 1?
pool.desc: Lithium has 2 shells and 1 valence electrons.

pool.q: In which horizontal Period is Phosphorus located?
pool.desc: Phosphorus is found in Period 3, Group 5.

pool.q: Identify the element found precisely in Group 2, Period 3.
pool.desc: Moving down to Period 3 and across to Group 2 reveals Magnesium.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 5?
pool.desc: Phosphorus has 3 shells and 5 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 5?
pool.desc: Nitrogen has 2 shells and 5 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 4, Group 1?
pool.desc: Potassium has 4 shells and 1 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 3?
pool.desc: Boron has 2 shells and 3 valence electrons.

pool.q: An element is in Group 6, Period 3. Name this element.
pool.desc: Sulfur sits directly beneath Oxygen in Period 3.

pool.q: In which horizontal Period is Potassium located?
pool.desc: Potassium is the first element in Period 4.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 4, Group 2?
pool.desc: Calcium has 4 shells and 2 valence electrons.

pool.q: Identify the element found in Group 2, Period 2.
pool.desc: Beryllium is the top element of Group 2 in Period 2.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 1, Group 8?
pool.desc: Helium has 1 shells and 8 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 6?
pool.desc: Oxygen has 2 shells and 6 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 6?
pool.desc: Sulfur has 3 shells and 6 valence electrons.

pool.q: In which Group number is Helium located?
pool.desc: Helium is grouped with noble gases in Group 8.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 2, Group 4?
pool.desc: Carbon has 2 shells and 4 valence electrons.

pool.q: Which element is located in Period 3, Group 8?
pool.desc: Argon has 3 shells and 8 valence electrons.

pool.q: In which horizontal Period is Lithium located?
pool.desc: Lithium is the first element of Period 2.

pool.q: Which element has a higher boiling point: Fluorine (F) or Iodine (I)?
pool.desc: Iodine molecules are larger with stronger intermolecular forces.

pool.q: Which is more reactive with water: Potassium or Sodium?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1. Potassium is lower in the group.

pool.q: Which is more reactive with water: Potassium or Lithium?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1. Potassium is lower in the group.

pool.q: Which element reacts more vigorously with cold water: Sodium (Na) or Lithium (Li)?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1; Sodium is lower down than Lithium.

pool.q: Which ion has a smaller ionic radius: Na+ or Mg2+?
pool.desc: Mg2+ has more protons pulling the same number of electrons closer.

pool.q: Which is more reactive with water: Lithium or Sodium?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1. Sodium is lower in the group.

pool.q: Which is more reactive with water: Sodium or Lithium?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1. Sodium is lower in the group.

pool.q: Which metal has a lower first ionization energy: Lithium (Li) or Potassium (K)?
pool.desc: Potassium's outer electron is further from the nucleus, making it easier to remove.

pool.q: Which is more reactive with water: Sodium or Potassium?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1. Potassium is lower in the group.

pool.q: Which atom has more occupied electron shells: Magnesium (Mg) or Calcium (Ca)?
pool.desc: Calcium has 4 shells (Period 4) while Magnesium only has 3 (Period 3).

pool.q: Which is more reactive with water: Lithium or Potassium?
pool.desc: Reactivity increases down Group 1. Potassium is lower in the group.

pool.q: Which atom has a higher first ionization energy: Helium (He) or Neon (Ne)?
pool.desc: Helium's outer electrons are closer to the nucleus with zero inner shell shielding.

pool.q: Which element is more non-metallic in its properties: Silicon (Si) or Chlorine (Cl)?
pool.desc: Non-metallic character increases from left to right across a period.

pool.q: Which element has a higher electrical conductivity: Aluminium (Al) or Sulfur (S)?
pool.desc: Aluminium is a metal with a sea of delocalised electrons.

pool.q: Which element has a higher shielding effect: Oxygen (O) or Sulfur (S)?
pool.desc: Sulfur has more filled inner electron shells to shield its outer electrons.

pool.q: Which element is more electronegative: Fluorine (F) or Oxygen (O)?
pool.desc: Fluorine is the most electronegative element on the entire periodic table.

pool.q: What trend is observed for melting points down Group 7 (Halogens)?
pool.desc: Down Group 7, molecules get larger, creating stronger intermolecular forces.

pool.q: As you move DOWN Group 7 (Halogens), what happens to the reactivity of the elements?
pool.desc: Increased shielding down the group makes it harder to attract an incoming electron.

pool.q: Why does nuclear charge increase across a period?
pool.desc: Each subsequent element adds one proton to the nucleus.

pool.q: Briefly state why Noble Gases (Group 8) are extremely unreactive.
pool.a: They have full outer electron shells
pool.desc: A complete outer shell provides maximum stability.
opts: They have full outer electron shells

pool.q: Which element is MORE reactive when reacting with water: Lithium (Li) or Potassium (K)?
pool.desc: Group 1 reactivity increases downwards because the outer electron is lost more easily.

pool.q: Which atom has a LARGER atomic radius: Sodium (Na) or Chlorine (Cl)?
pool.desc: Atomic radius decreases across a period due to increasing nuclear charge pulling the same shells closer.

pool.q: Which corner of the periodic table contains elements with the highest electronegativity?
pool.desc: Excluding noble gases, the top right (Fluorine) has the highest electronegativity.

pool.q: What is the general trend in metallic character as you move from left to right across Period 3?
pool.desc: Elements transition from metals on the left to non-metals on the right.

pool.q: What trend is observed for melting points down Group 1 (Alkali Metals)?
pool.desc: Positive ions get larger down the group, weakening the metallic bond attraction.

pool.q: Which region of the periodic table houses the atoms with the largest atomic radius?
pool.desc: Atomic radius increases moving down groups and moving right to left.

pool.q: Four 3D Bohr models of a Sodium atom are shown. Model A has configuration 2,8,1. Model B has 2,9. Model C has 2,8,2. Model D has 1,8,2. Which model is correct?
pool.desc: Sodium must have 11 total electrons arranged strictly as 2,8,1.

pool.q: A student draws a diagram for an Oxygen ion (O2-). Model A shows 6 outer electrons. Model B shows 8 outer electrons and a 2- charge. Model C shows 8 outer electrons and a 2+ charge. Which is correct?
pool.desc: An oxide ion has gained 2 electrons to complete its octet (8), giving it a net 2- charge.

pool.q: Three models show a Magnesium ion (Mg2+). Model A has 3 electron shells occupied. Model B has only 2 electron shells occupied and a 2+ charge. Model C has 2 shells and a 2- charge. Which is correct?
pool.desc: Mg (2,8,2) loses its 2 outer valence electrons, stripping it down to 2 full inner shells with a 2+ charge.

pool.q: A 3D model of a Methane molecule (CH4) shows Carbon at the center sharing 1 electron with each of the four Hydrogen atoms. What is the structural error?
pool.a: Carbon must share pairs of electrons
opts: It should be an ionic lattice
Carbon must share pairs of electrons
pool.desc: Each single covalent bond consists of a shared pair of electrons (one from C, one from H).

pool.q: A diagram shows a Chlorine molecule (Cl2). Model A shows a double covalent bond shared. Model B shows a single covalent bond where 1 electron pair is shared. Model C shows a transfer of electrons. Which is correct?
pool.desc: Chlorine atoms require only 1 shared electron pair to finish their octets, forming a single covalent bond.

pool.q: A diagram of Fluorine gas shows a shared pair of electrons, but one Fluorine atom has 9 remaining unshared valence electrons. What is the error?
pool.a: It has too many unshared electrons
opts: It has too many unshared electrons
It should be an ionic model
pool.desc: An unshared pool should only hold 6 electrons per atom, total 7 outer electrons including the shared one.

pool.q: A Lewis structure of Nitrogen gas (N2) shows a double bond. What is the structural error?
pool.desc: Nitrogen atoms must share 3 pairs of electrons (triple bond) to achieve full outer shells.

pool.q: A 3D model of a Lithium atom shows 3 protons and 4 neutrons in the core, with 3 electrons in the first shell. What is the error?
pool.a: Too many electrons in the first shell
opts: Too many electrons in the first shell
pool.desc: The first innermost electron shell can only accommodate a maximum of 2 electrons.

pool.q: A diagram of Helium shows two protons in the nucleus and two electrons spinning in separate outer valence shells. What is the error?
pool.a: Both electrons must be in the same inner shell
opts: Both electrons must be in the same inner shell
pool.desc: Helium's 2 electrons reside in the exact same innermost shell (n=1).

pool.q: A diagram of a Hydrogen molecule (H2) shows two spheres sharing four electrons between them. What is the error?
pool.desc: Each Hydrogen atom contributes 1 electron, making a total of 2 shared electrons (a single covalent bond).
